title_en: Chapter on Tawhid and Its Virtue
title_ar: باب فضل التوحيد وما يكفّر من الذنوب
content_en:
Tawhid — the absolute monotheistic belief in the oneness of Allah — is the foundational pillar upon which all of Islam rests. The opening chapter of Kitab al-Tawhid by Shaykh Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab establishes this principle by drawing directly from the Book of Allah and the authenticated Sunnah of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

The author begins by citing the statement of Allah (Glory be to Him): 'And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me' (Quran 51:56). This verse encapsulates the entire purpose of human existence — that worship, in its complete and undistorted form, belongs exclusively to Allah alone. True worship cannot be rendered to any intermediary, saint, or created being; it is a right that belongs to Allah by virtue of His being the Creator, the Sustainer, and the Sovereign of all that exists.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab then cites the verse: 'And verily We have sent among every nation a messenger (proclaiming): Worship Allah and avoid the Taghut (false deities)' (Quran 16:36). This verse establishes a crucial theological point: every prophet throughout history was sent with the same core message of Tawhid. From Nuh (Noah) to Ibrahim (Abraham) to Musa (Moses) to Isa (Jesus), and finally to Muhammad ﷺ, the call was one and the same — worship Allah alone and reject all false objects of worship.

The chapter also draws on the hadith narrated by Mu'adh ibn Jabal, in which the Prophet ﷺ told him: 'Do you know what the right of Allah upon His servants is?' Mu'adh replied that Allah and His Messenger knew best. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'His right upon them is that they worship Him and do not associate anything with Him. And do you know what their right upon Him is? Their right upon Him is that He will not punish whoever does not associate anything with Him.' This hadith, recorded in both al-Bukhari and Muslim, is a cornerstone of the chapter, demonstrating the bilateral covenant established through Tawhid.

The virtue of Tawhid lies not only in its theological correctness but also in its transformative power over a person's entire life. When a believer truly internalizes Tawhid, they are freed from every form of servitude except servitude to Allah. Fear of other than Allah dissipates. Reliance upon created beings diminishes. The heart finds its only true anchor in the Lord of all worlds. This internal liberation is one of the greatest gifts of Tawhid, and it is precisely this reality that the author seeks to impress upon the reader from the very first pages of his work.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's methodology throughout this chapter is distinctly textualist — he does not argue philosophically for the existence of God or the rationality of monotheism, but anchors every claim in direct Quranic verses and prophetic hadiths. This approach reflects the broader Athari method of grounding theological conclusions in the explicit texts of revelation rather than speculative reason alone. The primacy of Tawhid in this work signals to the reader that every subsequent chapter is, in essence, an elaboration of what worshipping Allah alone truly means in practice.
